title: Constraint Arbitrage

content.statement: Constraints can be exploited strategically when others treat them only as limitations.
content.question: What's a market-specific constraint (licensing, geography, culture) that competitors hate, but you can weaponize?
content.equation: CA = (Competitor Handicap - Your Handicap) × Market Value of Difference
sop: List every constraint specific to your market: local regulation, geography, licensing, culture, seasonality.
For each, note whether it's a pure cost (hurts everyone equally) or an asymmetric cost (hurts outsiders more than you).
Double down on the constraints that hurt competitors more, make them part of your pitch, not something you apologize for.
Rate your Competitor Handicap and Your Handicap 1-10 for the constraint, and rate the Market Value of the Difference 1-10 (see calculation, page 5)
Watch for new entrants, an asymmetric constraint today can erode if a competitor learns to navigate it too.
content.explainer: Local, regulated, or niche constraints are moats in disguise. Others flee them; you lean in.
questions: What's a market-specific constraint (licensing, geography, culture) that competitors hate, but you can weaponize?
What rule, regulation, or local quirk do most of your competitors try to avoid instead of build around?
If a national or online competitor tried to copy you here, what constraint would slow them down more than it slows you?
content.equation_example: Rate Competitor Handicap 1-10 (how much the constraint hurts them) and Your Handicap 1-10 (how much it hurts you), then rate the Market Value of the Difference 1-10. Competitor Handicap 8, Your Handicap 2, difference 6, Market Value of Difference 7 → CA = 6 × 7 = 42. Higher CA means the constraint is a real moat working in your favor; a CA near 0 means it's just a cost everyone shares equally.
content.closing_statement: The constraint they run from is your edge.

caption:
Principle Nº 38: Constraint Arbitrage

Constraints can be exploited strategically when others treat them only as limitations. Local, regulated, or niche constraints are moats in disguise.

Ask yourself: What's a market-specific constraint (licensing, geography, culture) that competitors hate, but you can weaponize?
